generated_comment: Arrey, itni saanwli hai... bilkul uske papa pe gayi hai. *sighs softly* Chalo, koi baat nahi — we'll put her on good milk, keep her out of the sun, and when she's older, ubtan every day. These days there are good creams also. Ladki hai, toh thoda dhyan rakhna padega na.
label: misogynistic or non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Anita frames the newborn's darker skin tone as a problem requiring remediation — creams, ubtan, avoiding sun — and explicitly links this urgency to the child's gender ('Ladki hai'), encoding colorism as a gendered burden placed on girls from birth. The comment is covertly misogynistic because Anita presents it as loving, practical caregiving rather than as an imposition of a discriminatory beauty standard.
persona_text: You are Anita. Age 50. Married. A homemaker in Delhi. Your daughter in law Kavya has just given birth to a baby girl. The baby is dark skinned like her father's side of the family. You are in the hospital room with the new parents. You are holding the baby. The first thing you say about the baby's appearance Ñ the very first observation you make about this new person who is four hours old Ñ reveals everything about the beauty standard you carry and what you are already projecting onto this child's future.
